title: Black Box Data After a Semi Crash in Seattle, Washington
twitter: Semi crash in Seattle? The truck's black box data disappears FAST. Washington gives you 2 years — but that evidence won't wait. Call 855 WRECKMATCH (855-897-3256) now. https://www.wreckmatch.com/blog/black-box-data-after-a-semi-crash-in-seattle-washington
linkedin:
If you or someone you know was involved in a semi-truck crash in Seattle, Washington, here's something most people don't realize: the most powerful evidence in your case may already be disappearing.

Commercial trucks are equipped with Electronic Control Modules (ECM) and Electronic Logging Devices (ELD) — commonly called black boxes. These systems record speed, braking patterns, engine data, hours of service, and GPS location in the moments before a crash. In serious truck accident claims, this data can be the difference between a strong case and a dismissed one.

The problem? Trucking companies are not obligated to preserve this data indefinitely. Without a formal legal hold or spoliation letter, it can be overwritten within days or weeks.

Washington State gives injured parties a 2-year statute of limitations — but waiting even a few weeks can mean critical evidence is gone forever.

At WreckMatch, we connect truck accident victims across Seattle and Washington State with attorneys from our network of 800+ law firms who understand exactly how to preserve ECM and ELD data, issue timely preservation demands, and build cases that hold carriers accountable.

Don't let the evidence disappear before your case begins. Submit your case now: https://www.wreckmatch.com/#form
facebook:
If you were hit by a semi-truck in Seattle, you're probably dealing with a lot right now — injuries, insurance calls, missed work, and unanswered questions about what really happened.

Here's something important to know: commercial trucks carry a 'black box' that records speed, braking, engine performance, and driver hours right before a crash. This data can prove exactly what the truck driver was doing — but trucking companies aren't required to keep it forever. It can be erased or overwritten within days.

Washington gives you 2 years to file a claim, but the evidence won't last that long. Acting quickly is the only way to make sure nothing gets lost before an attorney can step in and issue a preservation demand.

You deserve answers — and the right attorney can help you get them before the trail goes cold.

Have you or someone you love been in a semi-truck accident and wondered whether critical evidence was ever preserved?
reddit_title: Semi-truck hit me in Seattle — how do I make sure the truck's black box data doesn't get deleted before I can use it?
reddit_body:
After a semi-truck crash in Washington State, one of the most overlooked pieces of evidence is the truck's onboard data — specifically the ECM (Electronic Control Module) and ELD (Electronic Logging Device). These systems record speed, hard braking events, throttle position, engine RPM, GPS location, and hours-of-service logs leading up to the crash. In litigation, this data is often the clearest picture of what the driver was actually doing.

The catch is that trucking companies aren't legally required to preserve this data indefinitely. Without a formal spoliation letter or litigation hold sent directly to the carrier, the data can be automatically overwritten in as little as 30 days depending on the system. Washington's 2-year statute of limitations sounds like plenty of time, but the evidence window is much shorter — sometimes measured in days after the crash.

If you're in this situation, your first move should be getting an attorney to issue a preservation demand immediately — before you even think about talking to the trucking company's insurance. A resource like wreckmatch.com can connect you with truck accident attorneys in Washington who know exactly how to handle this step and have experience going up against large carriers and their legal teams.
